content.problemStatement:
Dazzler had an array of NNN **distinct** non-negative integers. Somehow he lost the array, but he knows the bitwise XOR of all the elements in the array. You have to help him to retrieve the array.

You are given two positive integers NNN and XXX. Construct an array AAA of NNN elements with the following conditions:


For each iii (1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N), 0≤Ai≤5⋅1050 \le A_i \le 5\cdot 10^50≤Ai​≤5⋅105.
All the elements in the array AAA should be **pairwise distinct**, i.e, Ai≠AjA_i \neq A_jAi​=Aj​ if i≠ji \neq ji=j
The bitwise XOR of all the NNN elements in the array should be equal to XXX, i.e, A1⊕A2⊕…⊕AN=XA_1 \oplus A_2 \oplus \ldots \oplus A_N = XA1​⊕A2​⊕…⊕AN​=X, where ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes the bitwise XOR operation.
If there are multiple possible solutions, you may print **any** of them.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test
cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and XXX — the size of the array and the bitwise XOR of the entire array.


Output Format
For each test case, output the NNN **distinct** non-negative integers satisfying the constraints above.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 3⋅1053\cdot 10^53⋅105

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 222: [7,3][7, 3][7,3] is one possible array, because 7⊕3=47 \oplus 3 = 47⊕3=4
Test case 333: [5,6,2][5, 6, 2][5,6,2] is one possible array, because 5⊕6⊕2=15 \oplus 6 \oplus 2 = 15⊕6⊕2=1. Another valid array is [8,20,29][8, 20, 29][8,20,29].
